items.title: NIA-AA 2018 Criteria
items.contentHtml:
NIA-AA diagnostic criteria (2018)

  • They are based on the biological construct: AT(N) (A: beta-amyloid, T: phosphorylated tau, N: neurodegeneration).
  • Categorized into: pathological Alzheimer's (A+T+), changes Alzheimer (A+T-), and non-Alzheimer (A-T-).
  • Clinical diagnosis: requires documented cognitive impairment plus positive biomarkers (amyloid positron emission tomography or cerebrospinal fluid).
  • Cheating exam: does not require established dementia; apply to mild cognitive impairment (mild cognitive impairment) and preclinical phase.

items.title: Amyloid and tau deposition
items.contentHtml:
Beta-amyloid deposition and neurofibrillary tangles

  • Beta-amyloid: 42 aa peptide (Aβ42) aggregates in extracellular senile plaques; onset in neocortex.
  • Neurofibrillary tangles: formed by hyperphosphorylated tau intracellular; Braak stages (I-VI) follow, starting in entorhinal cortex.
  • Clinical correlation: tangle load correlates better with the degree of dementia than amyloid plaques.
  • Examination trap: Braak stage I-II = asymptomatic; >III = symptoms; >V = severe dementia.

items.foco: Hippocampal atrophy on magnetic resonance imaging
items.title: Hippocampal atrophy
items.contentHtml:
Atrophy of the hippocampus on magnetic resonance imaging

  • Key finding: atrophy of the hippocampus on T1 sequences, evaluated with Scheltens scale (0-4) or volumetrics.
  • Volume loss > 2-3% annually suggests Alzheimer's disease compared to normal aging.
  • Characteristic pattern: asymmetric medial temporal atrophy, with predominance in CA1 and subiculum.
  • Cheating examination: in dementia with Lewy bodies hippocampal atrophy is mild or absent; useful for differential diagnosis.

items.foco: APOE ε4 as a genetic risk factor
items.title: APOE ε4
items.contentHtml:
APOE ε4 as a genetic risk factor

  • APOE ε4 is the main genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer's; autosomal dose-dependent inheritance.
  • An ε4 allele increases the risk 3-4 times; two alleles increase it 8-12 times and advance onset < 65 years old.
  • Mechanism: reduces the elimination of beta-amyloid and promotes its aggregation.
  • Check test: APOE ε4 is not diagnostic; It only modifies risk. Not all carriers develop Alzheimer's.

items.title: Early episodic memory
items.contentHtml:
Decline in early episodic memory

  • Episodic memory (recent events, context) is the first cognitive function affected by damage to the hippocampus and entorhinal cortex.
  • Clinical: forgetting recent conversations, repetition of questions, temporal disorientation.
  • Evaluation: learning test Verbal (for example, RAVLT) shows a low free recall and recognition curve.
  • Cheating exam: in frontotemporal dementia episodic memory is initially preserved; differential diagnosis key.

items.title: MMSE
items.contentHtml:
Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score

  • MMSE evaluates orientation, registration, attention, calculation, recall, language and praxia; maximum score 30.
  • Cut-off points: >24 normal; 20-23 slight deterioration; 10-19 moderate; 10 severe.
  • Limited sensitivity for mild cognitive impairment; does not detect damage in pure episodic memory.
  • Exam trap: MMSE is influenced by age and schooling; adjust for educational level (for example, <20 in illiterate).

items.title: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors

  • First line in mild to moderate Alzheimer's: donepezil (5-10 mg/day), rivastigmine (3-12 mg/day patch or capsules), galantamine (8-24 mg/day).
  • Mechanism: they inhibit the acetylcholinesterase increasing levels of acetylcholine in synapses.
  • Adverse effects: nausea, vomiting, bradycardia (avoid in AV block without pacemakers).
  • Examination trap: donepezil is the only one approved for severe Alzheimer's; Do not use in pure vascular dementia.

items.foco: Use of memantine in moderate to severe disease
items.title: Memantine
items.contentHtml:
Use of memantine in moderate to severe disease

  • Memantine is a non-competitive antagonist of the NMDA glutamate receptor; reduces excitotoxicity.
  • Indicated in moderate to severe Alzheimer's (MMSE <15). Dose: titrate to 20 mg/day.
  • May be combined with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (for example, donepezil + memantine) for synergistic benefit.
  • Examination trap: not effective in mild Alzheimer's; minimal adverse effects (dizziness, constipation). Contraindicated in severe renal failure (CrCl <30).

items.title: Differential diagnosis: vascular dementia
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with vascular dementia

  • Vascular dementia: abrupt onset, gradual course, neurological focality (hemiparesis, aphasia), Ischemic Hachinski >7.
  • magnetic resonance imaging: lacunar infarcts, leukoaraiosis, microbleeds; no predominant hippocampal atrophy.
  • Alzheimer's: insidious onset, progressive course, hippocampal atrophy, APOE ε4 positive.
  • Examination trap: mixed dementia (Alzheimer's + vascular) is common; treat both components.

items.foco: Warning signs in mild cognitive impairment
items.title: Warning signs in mild cognitive impairment
items.contentHtml:
Alarm signs in mild cognitive impairment

  • Mild cognitive impairment: subjective cognitive complaint + objective deficit in a domain (memory, executive) without affecting daily activities.
  • Alarm signs of conversion to Alzheimer's: progressive episodic memory loss, hippocampal atrophy on magnetic resonance imaging, biomarkers A+T+.
  • Poor prognostic factors: APOE ε4, depression, low cognitive reserve.
  • Examination trap: amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI) has > 50% conversion to Alzheimer's in 5 years; Non-amnestic mild cognitive impairment suggests other dementias.

items.foco: DSM-5 criteria for major neurocognitive disorder
items.title: DSM-5 major neurocognitive disorder
items.contentHtml:
DSM-5 criteria for major neurocognitive disorder

  • Requires significant decline in one or more cognitive domains (attention, memory, language, executive, visuospatial, social).
  • Interference with daily activities (instrumental or basic).
  • Not explained by delirium or other disorder mental.
  • Specify: probable Alzheimer's if insidious onset, progressive course, and genetic evidence or biomarkers.
  • Cheating exam: DSM-5 unifies dementia and mild cognitive impairment under major and minor neurocognitive disorder respectively.

items.title: Prevention vascular factors
items.contentHtml:
Prevention: control of vascular factors

  • Modifiable vascular risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, smoking, obesity.
  • Strict control of blood pressure (goal <130/80 mmHg) reduces the risk of Alzheimer's and vascular dementia.
  • Regular aerobic exercise (>150 min/week) and Mediterranean diet (rich in antioxidants, omega-3) associated with a lower incidence.
  • Trap test: supplements (vitamin E, ginkgo biloba) have not demonstrated prevention in controlled trials; do not recommend.
